mission.title: Install and Configure Falco on Kubernetes
mission.description: Falco is a cloud native runtime security tool designed to detect and alert on abnormal behavior and potential security threats in real-time. Installing Falco enables you to enhance the security posture of your Kubernetes environment by monitoring system calls and integrating with your container runtime.

steps.title: Add the Falco Helm repository
steps.description:
Add the official Falco Helm chart repository to your Helm configuration with the following command:
```bash
helm repo add falco https://falcosecurity.github.io/charts
helm repo update
```

steps.title: Install Falco using Helm
steps.description:
Install Falco in the 'falco-system' namespace with the following command:
```bash
helm install falco falco/falco --namespace falco-system --create-namespace --version 0.43.0
```

steps.title: Verify the Falco installation
steps.description:
Check that the Falco pod is running successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n falco-system
```

steps.title: Configure resource limits for Falco
steps.description:
Update the Falco deployment to set resource limits. Use the following command to edit the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment falco -n falco-system
```
In the editor, add the following under the 'spec.template.spec.containers.resources' section:
```yaml
resources:
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
  requests:
    memory: "256Mi"
    cpu: "250m"
```

steps.title: Set up port forwarding for Falco
steps.description:
To access Falco's output, set up port forwarding with the following command:
```bash
kubectl port-forward -n falco-system svc/falco 6677:6677
```

resolution.summary: A successful installation of Falco will show the Falco pod running in the 'falco-system' namespace. You will also be able to access Falco's output through the port forwarding setup.
codeSnippets:
helm repo add falco https://falcosecurity.github.io/charts
helm repo update

resources:
  limits:
    memory: "512Mi"
    cpu: "500m"
  requests:
    memory: "256Mi"
    cpu: "250m"

uninstall.title: Remove the Helm release
uninstall.description:
Uninstall the Falco release from the 'falco-system' namespace using the following command:
```bash
helm uninstall falco --namespace falco-system
```

uninstall.title: Clean up CRDs and persistent resources
uninstall.description:
Delete any Custom Resource Definitions (CRDs) and persistent volume claims (PVCs) associated with Falco. Use the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete crd falcosecurity.org
kubectl delete pvc --all -n falco-system
```

uninstall.title: Remove namespace and verify
uninstall.description:
Delete the 'falco-system' namespace and verify its removal with the following commands:
```bash
kubectl delete namespace falco-system
kubectl get namespaces
```

upgrade.title: Backup current state
upgrade.description:
Before upgrading, back up the current configuration and state of Falco. You can do this by exporting the current deployment:
```bash
kubectl get deployment falco -n falco-system -o yaml > falco-backup.yaml
```

upgrade.title: Update repo and run upgrade command
upgrade.description:
Update the Helm repository and upgrade Falco to the latest version using the following commands:
```bash
helm repo update
helm upgrade falco falco/falco --namespace falco-system --version 0.44.0
```

upgrade.title: Verify the upgrade
upgrade.description:
Check that the Falco pod is running the new version successfully:
```bash
kubectl get pods -n falco-system
```

troubleshooting.title: Falco pod is not starting
troubleshooting.description:
If the Falco pod is in a 'CrashLoopBackOff' state, check the logs for errors:
```bash
kubectl logs -n falco-system deployment/falco
``` 
Look for any configuration issues or missing permissions.

troubleshooting.title: Falco alerts not triggering
troubleshooting.description:
If Falco is not generating alerts, ensure that the rules are correctly configured. Check the rules file:
```bash
kubectl exec -n falco-system -it $(kubectl get pods -n falco-system -l app=falco -o jsonpath='{.items[0].metadata.name}') -- cat /etc/falco/falco_rules.yaml
``` 
Make sure the rules are enabled and correctly defined.

troubleshooting.title: Port forwarding not working
troubleshooting.description:
If you cannot access Falco's output via port forwarding, ensure that the service is running:
```bash
kubectl get svc -n falco-system
``` 
If the service is not available, check the deployment status and logs.

troubleshooting.title: Resource limits causing OOMKilled
troubleshooting.description:
If the Falco pod is being killed due to out-of-memory (OOM) issues, consider increasing the memory limits in the deployment:
```bash
kubectl edit deployment falco -n falco-system
``` 
Adjust the 'resources.limits.memory' value accordingly.

prerequisites.description: Ensure you have a Kubernetes cluster running version 1.24 or higher and have Helm and kubectl installed on your local machine.
